### Related literature {#sec-literature}

This paper sits at the intersection of real options, strategic investment, structural credit risk, and the economics of AI. No existing model combines the features needed to capture the AI compute investment problem: real options under uncertainty, endogenous default, oligopoly competition, regime-switching demand, training-inference allocation, and diminishing returns. I focus here on the direct building blocks; see @dixit1994investment and @smit2004strategic for comprehensive surveys of real options and strategic investment, respectively.

The real options foundation rests on @mcdonald1986value, who establish the canonical model of optimal timing for irreversible investment, and @pindyck1988irreversible, who extends the analysis to capacity choice, showing that each marginal unit is valued as a separate option.^[Other foundational contributions include @brennan1985evaluating on contingent-claims valuation of real assets, @bernanke1983irreversibility on the "bad news principle," @majd1987time on time-to-build options, and @barilan1996investment on how construction lags can reverse the uncertainty-investment relationship.] The game-theoretic extension by @grenadier2002option shows that competition *drastically erodes* the option value of waiting, with firms investing at near-zero NPV thresholds rather than the large positive thresholds predicted by single-firm models.^[@grenadier1996strategic shows this produces "development cascades" and endogenous overbuilding. @back2009open demonstrate that preemption incentives are even stronger under closed-loop strategies. @weeds2002strategic analyzes strategic delay in R\&D competition; see also @fudenberg1985preemption for game-theoretic foundations.] @huisman2015strategic---the closest single paper to the present model among the strategic models---study a duopoly in which firms choose *both* investment timing and capacity level under demand uncertainty. I extend their framework by incorporating endogenous default risk, training-inference allocation, diminishing returns, and regime switching with an endogenous arrival rate. @bouis2009multistage extend real options games to $N \geq 3$ firms, providing the sequential entry structure I use in @sec-extensions.^[@aguerrevere2003equilibrium and @kulatilaka1998strategic study how operating flexibility and strategic commitment can reverse the standard uncertainty-investment relationship in oligopoly settings.]

The structural credit risk literature originating with @merton1974pricing and @leland1994corporate provides the default mechanism.^[@blackcox1976valuing develop first-passage default models; @goldstein2001ebit extend to dynamic settings with endogenous default boundaries.] The most closely related papers combining investment and default are @hackbarth2014capital, who integrate real options, endogenous default, product market competition, and demand uncertainty, and @kumar2018optimal, who jointly optimize leverage and irreversible capacity, showing that leverage and capacity are *substitutes*.^[@sundaresan2015dynamic model sequential growth options with default risk, showing that high existing debt induces premature option exercise (risk-shifting). @bolton2011unified and @bolton2019investment model financially constrained firms. @hennessy2004tobin demonstrates that debt overhang is more severe for long-lived assets. @mauer1994interactions, @childs2005interactions, and @miao2005optimal further develop the interaction between financing frictions and investment distortions.] The present model integrates endogenous default into the strategic capacity-choice game of @huisman2015strategic, building on @hackbarth2012corporate for the joint investment-financing framework. @novymarx2007operating shows that operating leverage---the fixed-cost component of production---amplifies the systematic risk of firms with high operating costs, directly relevant to AI infrastructure firms with substantial power and maintenance expenses (captured by the operating cost parameter $\delta$ in the model).

@guo2005investment allow the demand growth rate and volatility to shift between regimes at Markov-switching times, showing that investment features large jumps when a favorable regime arrives---which I use to model the binary nature of AI demand uncertainty.^[@bloom2009impact develops a structural model of time-varying volatility shocks; @bloom2007uncertainty provide empirical validation of real options theory using firm-level panel data. @rob2003foreign model the choice between flexible high-marginal-cost production and irreversible low-marginal-cost capacity under demand uncertainty, mapping to the cloud-versus-owned compute decision. @jovanovic2005general document S-curve diffusion patterns for general-purpose technologies.] The economics of AI training exhibits power-law relationships between compute and capability [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training], motivating the concave production function. @jones1995rdbased generalizes endogenous growth to diminishing returns in idea production, and @bloom2020ideas document declining research productivity across domains---the closest analog to neural scaling laws.^[On the training-inference allocation, @akcigit2018growth study exploitation versus exploration R\&D in multi-product firms, and @klette2004innovating model firms as collections of production units subject to creative destruction. @berk1999optimal and @carlson2004corporate develop the growth option decomposition used in the valuation analysis. @aghion1992model, @grossman1991quality, and @kortum1997research provide Schumpeterian foundations for quality-ladder dynamics with diminishing returns.] On the macroeconomics of AI, @acemoglu2024simple develops a framework for assessing AI's impact on growth that highlights the importance of task automation rates, @korinek2024scenarios analyzes transition scenarios to AGI, and @jones2024agi models the growth implications of increasingly capable AI systems.^[@dellaseta2012optimal combine real options with learning curves; @nordhaus2021spirit and @aghion2018ai study whether AI could generate an economic singularity.]

The present paper makes four advances relative to this literature. First, it places the *training-inference allocation* at the center of the investment problem, with regime-specific competition (inference today, training quality tomorrow) and an *endogenous regime switch* in which firms' aggregate training compute accelerates the arrival of transformative AI. The exploitation-exploration trade-off in @akcigit2018growth is the closest analog, but applied here to compute allocation under real options with strategic interaction rather than R\&D portfolio choice. Second, it integrates *endogenous default risk with strategic capacity choice and training allocation*, showing that a *faith-based survival* mechanism---optimism about AI backed by training investment---lowers the default boundary. This combination is absent from existing work, which either models default without competition [@kumar2018optimal; @sundaresan2015dynamic] or competition without default [@grenadier2002option; @huisman2015strategic]. Third, the *revealed beliefs* methodology inverts the structural model to extract private information from observable investment decisions, using both investment intensity and training fraction as identifying moments---extending revealed preference theory [@samuelson1938note] to a dynamic setting with strategic interaction and multi-dimensional signals. Fourth, the application to AI infrastructure connects the corporate finance and industrial organization literatures to a sector where the magnitudes---\$660 billion in annual CapEx, order-of-magnitude belief dispersion---make the theoretical mechanisms quantitatively important.
